name: Footway Presence
definition: Assess whether a designated physical footway (sidewalk, paved path, or segregated pedestrian track) exists along the street segment.
evidence: Visible paved sidewalk, concrete path, asphalt footway, or physically segregated pedestrian corridor separated from the vehicular carriageway.
positiveExamples: Concrete elevated sidewalk alongside residential street.
Asphalt paved path separated by green strip.
negativeExamples: Dirt path showing wear but with no official segregation or paving.
Pedestrians sharing vehicle traffic lanes with no line or barrier separation.
unknownRule: Rate as Unknown if the camera angle is completely blocked by vehicles, dense vegetation, or extreme lens flare such that existence of the path cannot be verified.

name: Usable Clear Path (0.5m)
definition: Determine whether there is a continuous unobstructed walking path of at least 0.5 meters wide along the footway.
evidence: Measure clearance between structures, trees, utility poles, signposts, parking, or vending and the edge of the footway.
positiveExamples: Clear, wide open concrete sidewalk with no objects.
Wide sidewalk with trees planted in grates, leaving >1.0m walking path.
negativeExamples: Sidewalk completely blocked by utility box, forcing pedestrians to step down.
Informal goods or motorbikes parked across the entire width of the footway.
unknownRule: Mark Unknown if the clear width cannot be determined due to distance from camera or severe visual obstruction.

requires.disableMessage: Only applicable when a footway is present.

name: Effective Width
definition: The average usable width of the pedestrian space, excluding obstructions, measured in bands.
evidence: Estimate the physical width available for walking by comparing with standard human dimensions (e.g., shoulder width is ~0.5m, standard wheelchair is ~0.7m).
positiveExamples: Wide commercial sidewalks allowing 3+ people to walk abreast (> 2.0m).
Standard residential sidewalk allowing 2 people to pass comfortably (1.5m - 2.0m).
negativeExamples: Narrow concrete strip squeezed between wall and road drain (< 1.0m).
unknownRule: Use Unknown if high-angle perspective distortion makes width scaling unreliable.

requires.disableMessage: Only applicable when a footway is present.

name: Footway Continuity
definition: Assess whether the footway is a continuous paved facility along the observable segment, or if it breaks, terminates abruptly, or turns into an unpaved surface.
evidence: Observe pavement transitions, construction breaks, gaps where the footway vanishes into driveways, or unpaved mud sections.
positiveExamples: Continuous concrete pavement stretching from intersection to intersection.
Unbroken paved sidewalk with well-integrated standard driveway ramps.
negativeExamples: Sidewalk that ends suddenly in a pile of gravel, forcing pedestrians onto the street.
Gaps of more than 5 meters of dirt/gravel within an otherwise paved street.
unknownRule: Mark Unknown if the path disappears behind a building curve or dense vegetation at mid-block.

requires.disableMessage: Only applicable when a footway is present.

name: Surface Condition
definition: Rate the overall physical maintenance and structural state of the footway paving surface.
evidence: Look for cracked concrete panels, deep potholes, loose paving stones, severe root heave, pooled water, or muddy segments.
positiveExamples: Freshly laid asphalt or uniform interlocking brick pavers with zero cracks (Good).
Minor hairline cracks, stable joint gaps with no tripping hazards (Fair).
negativeExamples: Shattered concrete slabs, grass growing out of deep trenches, puddles covering the path, trip hazards >2cm (Poor).
unknownRule: Select Unknown if the sidewalk surface is completely obscured by a thick carpet of autumn leaves, snow, sand, or shadows.

requires.disableMessage: Only applicable when a footway is present.

name: Crossing Presence
definition: Evaluate if there is a marked pedestrian crossing (zebra lines, parallel lines, or highlighted pavement) visible within the segment views.
evidence: Paint markings on road surface, pedestrian crossing signs, or electronic pedestrian signals at junctions.
positiveExamples: Clearly painted white zebra stripes across the asphalt.
Tactile paved crossing ramp with pedestrian lights.
negativeExamples: An informal crossing point where pedestrians cross the busy road without markings.
A regular intersection with no pedestrian signage or street paint.
unknownRule: Rate Unknown if the intersection area is completely out of view or severely shadowed.

name: Encroachment Severity
definition: Our flagship metric. Rates the degree of pedestrian space loss due to temporary or permanent physical encroachments (vending stalls, commercial spillover, construction material, parked vehicles).
evidence: Observe physical structures, objects, shop displays, chairs/tables, building materials, or debris resting on the footway.
positiveExamples: Zero encroachments; the entire paved path is wide open (Level 1).
A single trash can or small potted plant slightly narrowing the path (Level 2).
negativeExamples: Multiple dining tables and a cooking stove occupying 80% of the pavement width (Level 4).
A metal gate, construction fence, or massive vendor tent blocking 100% of the path (Level 5).
unknownRule: Only use Unknown if the sidewalk space is completely behind a wall or truck, preventing any view.

requires.disableMessage: Only applicable when a footway is present.

name: Parking on Footway
definition: Assess whether motor vehicles, motorbikes, or bicycles are parked on top of the designated pedestrian footway space.
evidence: Cars, scooters, motorcycles, or utility vans parked on the sidewalk paver stones or designated pedestrian track.
positiveExamples: All cars parked in roadside bays or garage driveways; zero sidewalk parking.
One scooter parked parallel at the very edge of a 3-meter-wide walk.
negativeExamples: Three SUVs parked diagonally fully occupying the pedestrian strip.
A long line of delivery motorbikes parked on the paved footway.
unknownRule: Rate Unknown if parked vehicles are visible but it is impossible to distinguish if they are on the road shoulder or the footway.

requires.disableMessage: Only applicable when a footway is present.

name: Vending Intensity
definition: Rate the presence and scale of street vendors, food kiosks, informal markets, or sidewalk merchandise displays.
evidence: Pushcarts, street kiosks, clothing racks, fruit stands, or table setups with vendors on the footway.
positiveExamples: Clean sidewalks with zero commercial activity (None).
A single newspaper stand or shoe shine chair that leaves ample clear space (Light).
negativeExamples: A bustling street market with double-sided vendor displays, clothing lines, and heavy foot traffic clustering around stands (Heavy).
unknownRule: Rate Unknown if dense pedestrian crowds completely block the ground-level view of the shops.

requires.disableMessage: Only applicable when a footway is present.

name: Pedestrian Displacement
definition: Observe whether pedestrians are forced to walk in the vehicular carriageway because the footway is absent, blocked, or heavily encroached upon.
evidence: People walking alongside vehicular traffic on the asphalt, specifically where they have to step around parked cars or vendor stalls.
positiveExamples: All pedestrians walking safely on an unobstructed sidewalk.
No pedestrians walking in the road.
negativeExamples: A parent pushing a stroller in the traffic lane because a parked vehicle blocks the sidewalk ramp (Forced).
Pedestrians walking in the traffic lane because there is no sidewalk provided (Forced).
unknownRule: Mark Unknown if no pedestrians are currently present in the scene and the physical obstructions do not make displacement mathematically certain.

name: Traffic Threat
definition: Estimate the threat to pedestrian safety from vehicular speed and volume based solely on observable physical street design cues.
evidence: Number of vehicular lanes, presence of speed humps, median dividers, street width, and density of heavy trucks or high-speed traffic.
positiveExamples: A narrow, single-lane, winding residential street with speed tables (Low).
A street with heavy stop-and-go congestion where speeds are extremely low (Low).
negativeExamples: A wide, multi-lane arterial road without traffic calming or crossing lights, where high-speed cars dominate (High).
unknownRule: Mark Unknown only if vehicular carriageway is entirely out of view.

name: Overall Walkability
definition: A comprehensive, holistic score (1-7) summarizing how comfortable, safe, and pleasant the segment is for walking.
evidence: Consolidated evaluation of paving quality, safety from traffic, level of shade/greenery, presence of obstructions, and general accessibility.
positiveExamples: A tree-lined boulevard, wide sidewalks, tactile pavers, physical separation, low traffic speed (7 - Excellent).
negativeExamples: A broken, narrow concrete strip littered with trash, blocked by vehicles, immediately adjacent to a high-speed highway with no barriers (1 - Very Poor).
unknownRule: Should never be marked Unknown unless the entire panorama is corrupt or unreadable.
